---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    font-family: 'Trebuchet MS'
    scrollable: true
---

<style>
body { font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif; }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal h4 { color: #C41E3A; }
.reveal { font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif; }
.reveal pre { font-size: 0.72em; }
.big-center { text-align:center; font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.6; margin-top: 30px; }

/* Pillar banner */
.pillar-banner {
  text-align:center;
  margin-top: 30px;
  padding: 28px;
  border-radius: 14px;
  background: rgba(196, 30, 58, 0.06);
  border: 2px solid #C41E3A;
}
.pillar-banner .num {
  font-size: 1.1em;
  letter-spacing: 3px;
  color: #8B0000;
  font-weight: 700;
}
.pillar-banner .name {
  font-size: 2.2em;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #C41E3A;
  margin: 8px 0;
}
.pillar-banner .tag {
  font-size: 1.1em;
  color: #555;
}

/* QCM Styles */
.qcm-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 14px;
  margin-top: 24px;
}
.qcm-option {
  background: #f0f4f8;
  border: 2px solid #bdc3c7;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  font-size: 0.95em;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: all 0.3s ease;
  text-align: left;
  font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif;
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 10px;
}
.qcm-option:hover { border-color: #C41E3A; background: #fbebed; }
.qcm-option.correct {
  background: #d5f5e3;
  border-color: #27ae60;
  color: #1e8449;
  font-weight: 700;
}
.qcm-option.wrong {
  background: #fde8e8;
  border-color: #e74c3c;
  color: #c0392b;
}
.qcm-option .badge {
  font-size: 1.2em;
  min-width: 24px;
}
.qcm-label {
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #C41E3A;
  margin-right: 4px;
}
.qcm-question {
  font-size: 1.25em;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: #2c3e50;
  background: #fbebed;
  border-left: 5px solid #C41E3A;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  margin-bottom: 6px;
}
.q-number {
  font-size: 0.85em;
  color: #7f8c8d;
  margin-bottom: 6px;
}
</style>

<script>
function qcm(optionEl, isCorrect) {
  const grid = optionEl.closest('.qcm-grid');
  if (grid.dataset.answered) return;
  grid.dataset.answered = "true";
  const opts = grid.querySelectorAll('.qcm-option');
  opts.forEach(o => {
    o.style.cursor = 'default';
    if (o === optionEl) {
      o.classList.add(isCorrect ? 'correct' : 'wrong');
      o.querySelector('.badge').textContent = isCorrect ? '✅' : '❌';
    } else if (o.dataset.correct === "true") {
      o.classList.add('correct');
      o.querySelector('.badge').textContent = '✅';
    }
  });
}
</script>

<div style="text-align:center; margin-top: 40px; line-height: 1.4;">
  <h1 style="font-size: 2.5em; font-weight: 700; color: #C41E3A; margin-bottom: 10px;">
    Programming Language II
  </h1>
  <h2 style="font-size: 1.6em; font-weight: 500; color: #555;">
    The Four Pillars of Object-Oriented Programming
  </h2>
  <div style="border-bottom: 4px solid #C41E3A; width: 45%; margin: 18px auto;"></div>
  <h3 style="margin-top: 25px; font-weight: 700; color: #050E3C">
    By: MIN Sothearith
  </h3>
  <h4 style="font-weight: 700; color: #666;">
    Phnom Penh International University
  </h4>
</div>

## Today's Session

Last week we learned how to **build a class** and make **objects**.

Today we go one step further: the **four pillars** that hold up all of OOP.

::: {.callout-note}
## Plan for today
1. **Review** -- what we studied last week (first, slowly)
2. **Pillar 1 -- Encapsulation** -- protecting data
3. **Pillar 2 -- Inheritance** -- reusing a class
4. **Pillar 3 -- Polymorphism** -- same action, different behaviour
5. **Pillar 4 -- Abstraction** -- hiding the complex details
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
We take each pillar **one at a time**. There is **no rush**. First we ask *what* it is, then *why* we need it.
:::

---

# Part 0 -- Review of Last Week

---

## Let's remember where we stopped

Before new ideas, let's make sure last week is **solid**.

Last week we answered four questions:

::: {.callout-note}
- **Why** do we need OOP?
- **What** is a class?
- **What** is an object?
- How do we **build** a class with data and behaviour?
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
The next few slides are a **quick refresher**. If any part feels unclear, stop me -- this is the foundation for today.
:::

## Review 1 -- Why OOP?

Without OOP, **data** and **actions** get separated and messy:

```python
dog1_name = "Rex"
dog1_age  = 3
dog2_name = "Bella"
dog2_age  = 5

def bark(name):
    print(f"{name} says Woof!")
```

::: {.callout-important}
The dog's data is in loose variables; the `bark` action is far away. Nothing keeps them together.
:::

<div class="big-center">
OOP bundles <strong>data + actions</strong> into one unit: an <strong>object</strong>.
</div>

## Review 2 -- Class = blueprint

A **class** is a **blueprint** -- a plan you write **once**.

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
```

| Blueprint (the plan) | Real things made from it |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| `Dog` class | Rex, Bella, Max |

::: {.callout-note}
The class is **not** a real dog. It is the *plan* used to make dogs.
:::

## Review 3 -- Object = the real thing

An **object** is a real thing **built** from the class.

```python
rex   = Dog("Rex", 3)      # object 1
bella = Dog("Bella", 5)    # object 2
```

- One blueprint → **many** objects
- Each object holds **its own** data
- Changing Rex's age does **not** change Bella's age

::: {.callout-note}
Another word for object is **instance**. "Rex is an instance of `Dog`."
:::

## Review 4 -- `__init__` and `self`

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
```

- `__init__` runs **automatically** when we create an object -- the **constructor**
- It **sets up** the starting data
- `self` means **"this particular object"** (this dog)

::: {.callout-important}
`self.name = name` stores the name **inside this dog**. Every method's first parameter is `self`.
:::

## Review 5 -- Attributes and methods

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name          # attributes = data
        self.age = age

    def bark(self):               # method = behaviour
        print(f"{self.name} says Woof!")
```

- **Attribute** = data stored in the object → read with `rex.name`
- **Method** = a function inside the class → the object's behaviour

```python
rex = Dog("Rex", 3)
print(rex.name)   # Rex
rex.bark()        # Rex says Woof!
```

## Review 6 -- Printing nicely with `__str__`

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

    def __str__(self):
        return f"Dog: {self.name}, age {self.age}"

rex = Dog("Rex", 3)
print(rex)        # Dog: Rex, age 3
```

::: {.callout-note}
`__str__` teaches the object **how to print itself**. It must **return** a string.
:::

## Review -- summary

| Idea | In one sentence |
|------|-----------------|
| **Why OOP** | Keep data and actions **together** |
| **Class** | A **blueprint** -- describe once |
| **Object** | A real thing **built** from the class |
| **`__init__`** | Runs automatically; **sets up** the data |
| **`self`** | Means **"this particular object"** |
| **Attribute** | Data in an object -- `obj.name` |
| **Method** | A function inside the class -- behaviour |
| **`__str__`** | Teaches the object to **print itself** |

::: {.callout-tip}
If this table makes sense, you are ready for today's four pillars. 👍
:::

## Quick check before we continue

<div class="q-number">Warm-up -- Last week</div>
<div class="qcm-question">In <code>rex = Dog("Rex", 3)</code>, what is <code>rex</code>?</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> A class (a blueprint)</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> An object (an instance of Dog)</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> A method</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> An attribute</button>
</div>

---

# The Four Pillars -- The Big Picture

---

## What are the "four pillars"?

A **pillar** is a column that **holds up** a building.

OOP stands on **four** of them. Every good class design uses these ideas.

::: {.callout-note}
## The four pillars
1. **Encapsulation** -- keep data safe inside the object
2. **Inheritance** -- a new class reuses an existing one
3. **Polymorphism** -- the same action behaves differently
4. **Abstraction** -- hide complex details, show a simple interface
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Don't memorise the four words yet. We will **build understanding** of each, one at a time.
:::

## How we will study each pillar

For **every** pillar we ask the same three questions:

::: {.callout-note}
1. **What** is it? (a simple definition)
2. **Why** do we need it? (the problem it solves)
3. **How** do we do it in Python? (a small example)
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Same recipe four times. Once the rhythm is familiar, each pillar feels easy.
:::

---

# Pillar 1 -- Encapsulation

---

## Pillar 1

<div class="pillar-banner">
  <div class="num">PILLAR 1 OF 4</div>
  <div class="name">Encapsulation</div>
  <div class="tag">Keep an object's data <strong>safe inside</strong> the object.</div>
</div>

::: {.callout-note}
The word comes from **"capsule"** -- a small container that holds something and protects it.
:::

## What is encapsulation?

<div class="big-center">
Encapsulation means <strong>bundling data with the methods</strong><br>that work on it, and <strong>controlling access</strong> to that data.
</div>

In simple words:

- The object keeps its data **inside**
- The outside world must go **through the object's methods** to change it
- The object can **protect itself** from bad data

::: {.callout-note}
Think of a **medicine capsule**: the powder is sealed inside. You don't touch it directly.
:::

## Why do we need it? -- the problem

Without protection, **anyone** can change the data to **anything**:

```python
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance

acc = BankAccount("Alice", 100)
acc.balance = -999999      # nothing stops this!
```

::: {.callout-important}
A bank balance should **never** become negative like this. But our code allows it. The data is **unprotected**.
:::

## Real-life analogy

Think of an **ATM machine**.

- You **cannot** open it and grab the cash directly
- You must use **buttons**: deposit, withdraw, check balance
- The machine **checks the rules** (enough balance? correct PIN?)

::: {.callout-note}
Encapsulation makes your object behave like an ATM: data is **inside**, access is through **safe methods**.
:::

## How: step 1 -- mark data as private

In Python, we put **two underscores** `__` in front of an attribute to make it **private**:

```python
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance):
        self.owner = owner
        self.__balance = balance      # private!  note the __
```

```python
acc = BankAccount("Alice", 100)
print(acc.__balance)     # AttributeError -- cannot reach it directly
```

::: {.callout-note}
The `__` tells everyone: *"this is private -- do not touch from outside."*
:::

## How: step 2 -- give safe methods to use it

If data is hidden, we provide **methods** to work with it **safely**:

```python
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance):
        self.owner = owner
        self.__balance = balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:                     # rule: must be positive
            self.__balance += amount

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if 0 < amount <= self.__balance:   # rule: cannot overdraw
            self.__balance -= amount

    def get_balance(self):                 # safe way to read it
        return self.__balance
```

## How: step 3 -- use it safely

```python
acc = BankAccount("Alice", 100)

acc.deposit(50)
print(acc.get_balance())    # 150

acc.withdraw(1000)          # ignored -- not enough balance
print(acc.get_balance())    # 150  (unchanged, protected!)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Now the balance can **only** change through rules we wrote. The object **protects itself**. That is encapsulation.
:::

## Encapsulation -- without vs with

| Without encapsulation | With encapsulation |
|-----------------------|--------------------|
| Data is public | Data is **private** (`__name`) |
| Anyone can set bad values | Methods **check the rules** |
| Object cannot protect itself | Object **stays valid** |

::: {.callout-note}
**Why we need it:** safety. The object guards its own data so the program can't break it by accident.
:::

---

# Pillar 2 -- Inheritance

---

## Pillar 2

<div class="pillar-banner">
  <div class="num">PILLAR 2 OF 4</div>
  <div class="name">Inheritance</div>
  <div class="tag">A new class <strong>reuses</strong> an existing class.</div>
</div>

::: {.callout-note}
Like a child **inheriting** features from a parent -- the new class gets the parent's data and methods for free.
:::

## What is inheritance?

<div class="big-center">
Inheritance lets a <strong>new class</strong> (child) reuse<br>everything from an <strong>existing class</strong> (parent).
</div>

- The **parent** class has the general features
- The **child** class gets them **automatically**
- The child can **add** new features or **change** old ones

::: {.callout-note}
Words you'll hear: **parent / child**, or **base / derived**, or **super / sub** class. They mean the same thing.
:::

## Why do we need it? -- the problem

Imagine a `Dog` and a `Cat`. Look at the **repetition**:

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def eat(self):
        print(f"{self.name} is eating.")
    def sleep(self):
        print(f"{self.name} is sleeping.")

class Cat:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def eat(self):                       # exactly the same!
        print(f"{self.name} is eating.")
    def sleep(self):                     # exactly the same!
        print(f"{self.name} is sleeping.")
```

::: {.callout-important}
`eat` and `sleep` are **copied**. If we fix a bug in one, we must remember the other. This breaks the **DRY** rule (Don't Repeat Yourself).
:::

## Real-life analogy

Think of **animals** in general.

- **All animals** can eat and sleep → put that in a parent `Animal`
- A **dog** is an animal that *also* barks
- A **cat** is an animal that *also* meows

::: {.callout-note}
We write the **shared** part once in `Animal`, then each child **adds** only what is special.
:::

## How: step 1 -- write the parent

Put the **shared** features in a parent class `Animal`:

```python
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def eat(self):
        print(f"{self.name} is eating.")

    def sleep(self):
        print(f"{self.name} is sleeping.")
```

::: {.callout-note}
Everything **every** animal can do lives here -- written **once**.
:::

## How: step 2 -- children inherit it

A child names its parent **in the parentheses**:

```python
class Dog(Animal):        # Dog inherits from Animal
    def bark(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Woof!")

class Cat(Animal):        # Cat inherits from Animal
    def meow(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Meow!")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
We did **not** rewrite `eat`, `sleep`, or `__init__`. The children got them **for free**.
:::

## How: step 3 -- use the children

```python
rex = Dog("Rex")
rex.eat()      # Rex is eating.    (inherited from Animal)
rex.bark()     # Rex says Woof!    (Dog's own method)

kitty = Cat("Kitty")
kitty.sleep()  # Kitty is sleeping.  (inherited)
kitty.meow()   # Kitty says Meow!    (Cat's own method)
```

::: {.callout-note}
Each child has **both** the inherited methods **and** its own special ones.
:::

## Adding to the parent's setup with `super()`

What if a `Dog` also needs a **breed**? We **extend** `__init__`:

```python
class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        super().__init__(name)     # run Animal's __init__ first
        self.breed = breed         # then add Dog's own data

rex = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")
print(rex.name)    # Rex      (set by Animal via super())
print(rex.breed)   # Labrador (set by Dog)
```

::: {.callout-note}
`super()` means **"the parent."** `super().__init__(name)` reuses the parent's setup instead of copying it.
:::

## Inheritance -- without vs with

| Without inheritance | With inheritance |
|---------------------|------------------|
| Copy shared code into every class | Write shared code **once** in the parent |
| Fix bugs in many places | Fix in **one** place |
| Classes unrelated | Clear **"is-a"** relationship (a Dog **is an** Animal) |

::: {.callout-note}
**Why we need it:** reuse. Share common code through a parent, and let each child add only what is special.
:::

---

# Pillar 3 -- Polymorphism

---

## Pillar 3

<div class="pillar-banner">
  <div class="num">PILLAR 3 OF 4</div>
  <div class="name">Polymorphism</div>
  <div class="tag">The <strong>same action</strong> behaves differently for different objects.</div>
</div>

::: {.callout-note}
The word means **"many shapes"**: *poly* = many, *morph* = shape. One name, many behaviours.
:::

## What is polymorphism?

<div class="big-center">
Polymorphism means we can call the <strong>same method name</strong><br>on different objects and each one <strong>does its own thing</strong>.
</div>

- We write `animal.speak()`
- A dog **barks**, a cat **meows**, a cow **moos**
- **Same call**, different result -- depending on the object

::: {.callout-note}
We don't need to know *which* animal it is. We just say `speak()` and trust each one to respond correctly.
:::

## Why do we need it? -- the problem

Without polymorphism, we **check the type** every time:

```python
def make_sound(animal):
    if animal.kind == "dog":
        print("Woof!")
    elif animal.kind == "cat":
        print("Meow!")
    elif animal.kind == "cow":
        print("Moo!")
    # ... a new animal? edit this function AGAIN
```

::: {.callout-important}
Every new animal forces us to **edit this function** and add another `elif`. Messy and easy to forget.
:::

## Real-life analogy

Think of a **"Play" button**.

- Press Play on a **song** → music plays
- Press Play on a **video** → video plays
- Press Play on a **game** → game starts

::: {.callout-note}
**Same button** ("Play"), but each thing knows **how to play itself**. You don't press a different button for each.
:::

## How: each class defines the same method

Give every class a method with the **same name**, `speak`:

```python
class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Woof!")

class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Meow!")

class Cow(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Moo!")
```

::: {.callout-note}
Same method **name** (`speak`), different **body** in each class. That is the heart of polymorphism.
:::

## How: one loop handles them all

```python
animals = [Dog("Rex"), Cat("Kitty"), Cow("Daisy")]

for animal in animals:
    animal.speak()      # same call -- each does its own thing
```

**Output:**
```
Rex says Woof!
Kitty says Meow!
Daisy says Moo!
```

::: {.callout-tip}
No `if/elif` checking the type! We just call `speak()` and each object responds correctly.
:::

## The big win: easy to extend

Add a brand-new animal -- **no existing code changes**:

```python
class Duck(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says Quack!")

animals.append(Duck("Donald"))

for animal in animals:
    animal.speak()      # the loop already works for Duck too!
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The loop never mentioned "Duck". Polymorphism let it work **automatically**.
:::

## Polymorphism -- without vs with

| Without polymorphism | With polymorphism |
|----------------------|-------------------|
| `if/elif` to check each type | Just call the **same method** |
| New type → edit old code | New type → **add a class**, nothing else |
| Fragile and long | Short and flexible |

::: {.callout-note}
**Why we need it:** flexibility. Treat different objects the **same way**, and let each decide how to respond.
:::

---

# Pillar 4 -- Abstraction

---

## Pillar 4

<div class="pillar-banner">
  <div class="num">PILLAR 4 OF 4</div>
  <div class="name">Abstraction</div>
  <div class="tag">Hide the complex details; show a <strong>simple interface</strong>.</div>
</div>

::: {.callout-note}
To **abstract** means to focus on **what** something does, and hide **how** it does it.
:::

## What is abstraction?

<div class="big-center">
Abstraction means showing only the <strong>essential</strong> features<br>and hiding the <strong>complicated inner workings</strong>.
</div>

- The user sees a **simple** set of actions
- The **complex code** is hidden inside
- You use the object **without** knowing how it works internally

::: {.callout-note}
Encapsulation hides the **data**. Abstraction hides the **complexity** -- the messy "how". They work together.
:::

## Why do we need it? -- real-life analogy

Think of **driving a car**.

- To drive, you use: the **steering wheel**, **pedals**, **gear**
- You do **not** need to understand fuel injection, spark timing, or the gearbox internals
- The complex engine is **hidden** behind a simple interface

::: {.callout-tip}
Abstraction lets us **use** powerful things through a **simple** set of controls -- without being experts in the internals.
:::

## Why in code? -- the problem

Imagine sending an email. The *real* steps are complex:

```text
connect to server → authenticate → format headers →
encode body → open SMTP socket → handle errors → close ...
```

::: {.callout-important}
If **every** programmer had to write all of that each time, code would be huge and full of mistakes.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Abstraction lets us hide all those steps behind **one simple method**: `send()`.
:::

## How: hide the steps behind a simple method

The complex steps live **inside**, as helper methods. The user sees only `send()`:

```python
class EmailSender:
    def send(self, to, message):       # the only thing users call
        self.__connect()
        self.__authenticate()
        self.__deliver(to, message)

    def __connect(self):        # hidden detail
        print("Connecting to server...")
    def __authenticate(self):   # hidden detail
        print("Logging in...")
    def __deliver(self, to, message):  # hidden detail
        print(f"Sending to {to}: {message}")
```

## How: the user's view is simple

```python
sender = EmailSender()
sender.send("alice@mail.com", "Hello!")
```

**Output:**
```
Connecting to server...
Logging in...
Sending to alice@mail.com: Hello!
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The user wrote **one line**. All the hard work is **hidden** behind `send()`. That is abstraction.
:::

## Enforcing abstraction with an abstract class

Python can **force** every child to provide a method using an **abstract base class**:

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Shape(ABC):                  # ABC = Abstract Base Class
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self):                # children MUST define this
        pass

class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, r):
        self.r = r
    def area(self):                # required -- so we provide it
        return 3.14 * self.r ** 2
```

::: {.callout-note}
`Shape` defines **what** every shape must do (`area`), but not **how**. Each child fills in the details.
:::

## Why an abstract class helps

```python
shape = Shape()        # Error: cannot create an abstract class

circle = Circle(5)
print(circle.area())   # 78.5
```

::: {.callout-important}
You **cannot** create a plain `Shape` -- it is only a **template**. Any child that forgets `area()` will also fail. The rule is **enforced**.
:::

## Abstraction -- without vs with

| Without abstraction | With abstraction |
|---------------------|------------------|
| User sees all the messy steps | User sees a **simple** method |
| Easy to use it wrong | Hard to misuse -- details hidden |
| Repeated complex code | Complexity written **once**, inside |

::: {.callout-note}
**Why we need it:** simplicity. Show a clean, easy interface and hide the complicated "how" inside.
:::

---

# Putting It All Together

---

## The four pillars side by side

| Pillar | One-line idea | Why we need it |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Encapsulation** | Keep data **private**, change it through methods | **Safety** |
| **Inheritance** | A child class **reuses** a parent | **Reuse** |
| **Polymorphism** | Same method, **different** behaviour | **Flexibility** |
| **Abstraction** | Hide the complex **"how"** | **Simplicity** |

::: {.callout-tip}
Four ideas, four reasons: **safe, reusable, flexible, simple** code.
:::

## How they work together

These pillars are **not** separate tools -- they cooperate:

::: {.callout-note}
- **Inheritance** builds a family: `Animal → Dog, Cat`
- **Polymorphism** lets us call `speak()` on the whole family
- **Encapsulation** keeps each animal's data safe inside
- **Abstraction** hides the messy details behind simple methods
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Good OOP design uses **all four** together, naturally.
:::

## A tiny example using all four

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Animal(ABC):                 # Abstraction: a template
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.__name = name         # Encapsulation: private data
    def get_name(self):
        return self.__name
    @abstractmethod
    def speak(self):               # children must define it
        pass

class Dog(Animal):                 # Inheritance: reuse Animal
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.get_name()} says Woof!")

class Cat(Animal):                 # Inheritance again
    def speak(self):
        print(f"{self.get_name()} says Meow!")

for a in [Dog("Rex"), Cat("Kitty")]:
    a.speak()                      # Polymorphism: same call
```

::: {.callout-tip}
All four pillars in one small program. This is what real OOP looks like.
:::

---

# Quick Quiz -- Check Yourself 🎯

---

## Q1 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 1 of 6 -- Encapsulation</div>
<div class="qcm-question">What does putting <code>__</code> before an attribute name do?</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> Deletes the attribute</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> Makes it private -- hidden from outside</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> Makes it run faster</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> Turns it into a method</button>
</div>

## Q2 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 2 of 6 -- Pillars</div>
<div class="qcm-question">Which pillar means "a new class reuses an existing class"?</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> Encapsulation</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> Inheritance</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> Polymorphism</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> Abstraction</button>
</div>

## Q3 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 3 of 6 -- Inheritance</div>
<div class="qcm-question">In <code>class Dog(Animal):</code>, what is <code>Animal</code>?</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> The parent (base) class</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> The child class</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> An object</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> A method</button>
</div>

## Q4 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 4 of 6 -- Inheritance</div>
<div class="qcm-question">What does <code>super().__init__(name)</code> do?</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> Creates a new object</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> Runs the parent class's <code>__init__</code></button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> Deletes the parent class</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> Makes the class abstract</button>
</div>

## Q5 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 5 of 6 -- Polymorphism</div>
<div class="qcm-question">Calling <code>speak()</code> on a Dog and a Cat gives different sounds. This is...</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> Encapsulation</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> Abstraction</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> Polymorphism</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> Inheritance</button>
</div>

## Q6 / 6

<div class="q-number">Question 6 of 6 -- Abstraction</div>
<div class="qcm-question">An <code>@abstractmethod</code> in an abstract base class means...</div>
<div class="qcm-grid">
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="true"  onclick="qcm(this, true)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">A.</span> Every child class must define that method</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">B.</span> The method is private</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">C.</span> The method runs automatically</button>
  <button class="qcm-option" data-correct="false" onclick="qcm(this, false)"><span class="badge">·</span><span class="qcm-label">D.</span> The method cannot have a body ever</button>
</div>

---

# Your Turn -- Practice 🛠️

---

## Practice -- How it works

Three short exercises -- **one pillar at a time**. Try each yourself first, then check the solution.

::: {.callout-tip}
Use the same habit as last week:
data in `__init__` → behaviour in methods → use `self` to reach the object's own data.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Take your time. It is fine to look back at the slides for help.
:::

---

# Exercise 1 -- Encapsulation

## A Protected `Temperature`

**Task:** Create a class `Temperature` that:

1. Stores a **private** attribute `__celsius` in `__init__`
2. Has a method `set_celsius(value)` that updates it **only if** the value is `-273` or higher (absolute zero)
3. Has a method `get_celsius()` that returns the current value

Create one object, try to set `-500` (should be **rejected**), then set `25`.

**Expected output:**
```
20
20
25
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Hint
Inside `set_celsius`, check `if value >= -273:` before storing. Use `self.__celsius` for the private data.
:::

## Exercise 1 -- Sample Solution

```python
class Temperature:
    def __init__(self, celsius):
        self.__celsius = celsius

    def set_celsius(self, value):
        if value >= -273:
            self.__celsius = value

    def get_celsius(self):
        return self.__celsius

t = Temperature(20)
print(t.get_celsius())   # 20
t.set_celsius(-500)      # rejected
print(t.get_celsius())   # 20  (unchanged)
t.set_celsius(25)
print(t.get_celsius())   # 25
```

**Pillar practiced:** Encapsulation -- private data + safe methods with a rule

---

# Exercise 2 -- Inheritance

## `Vehicle` → `Car`

**Task:**

1. Create a parent class `Vehicle` with `__init__(self, brand)` and a method `start()` that prints `"<brand> is starting."`
2. Create a child class `Car(Vehicle)` that adds `__init__(self, brand, doors)` -- use `super()` to set `brand`, then store `doors`
3. Add a method `honk()` that prints `"<brand> says Beep!"`

Create a `Car`, call `start()` (inherited) and `honk()` (its own).

**Expected output:**
```
Toyota is starting.
Toyota says Beep!
4
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Hint
In `Car.__init__`, call `super().__init__(brand)` first, then `self.doors = doors`.
:::

## Exercise 2 -- Sample Solution

```python
class Vehicle:
    def __init__(self, brand):
        self.brand = brand

    def start(self):
        print(f"{self.brand} is starting.")

class Car(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, brand, doors):
        super().__init__(brand)     # reuse parent's setup
        self.doors = doors

    def honk(self):
        print(f"{self.brand} says Beep!")

car = Car("Toyota", 4)
car.start()           # Toyota is starting.  (inherited)
car.honk()            # Toyota says Beep!     (own method)
print(car.doors)      # 4
```

**Pillar practiced:** Inheritance -- parent/child, `super()`, reusing methods

---

# Exercise 3 -- Polymorphism

## Many Shapes, One `area()`

**Task:**

1. Create three classes: `Square`, `Rectangle`, `Circle`
2. Each has an `__init__` for its measurements and a method `area()`
3. Put one of each in a list and **loop** through, printing each area -- using the **same** `area()` call

**Expected output:**
```
Square area: 16
Rectangle area: 24
Circle area: 78.5
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Hint
Square area = `side * side`. Rectangle = `width * height`. Circle = `3.14 * r * r`. The loop just calls `shape.area()`.
:::

## Exercise 3 -- Sample Solution

```python
class Square:
    def __init__(self, side):
        self.side = side
    def area(self):
        return self.side * self.side

class Rectangle:
    def __init__(self, w, h):
        self.w = w
        self.h = h
    def area(self):
        return self.w * self.h

class Circle:
    def __init__(self, r):
        self.r = r
    def area(self):
        return 3.14 * self.r * self.r

shapes = [Square(4), Rectangle(6, 4), Circle(5)]
names  = ["Square", "Rectangle", "Circle"]

for name, shape in zip(names, shapes):
    print(f"{name} area: {shape.area()}")
```

**Pillar practiced:** Polymorphism -- same method name, different behaviour, one loop

---

## What we learned today

| Pillar | What it is | Why we need it |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **Encapsulation** | Private data + safe methods | Keep data **safe** |
| **Inheritance** | Child reuses parent | Avoid **repeating** code |
| **Polymorphism** | Same call, different behaviour | Stay **flexible** |
| **Abstraction** | Hide the complex details | Keep it **simple** |

::: {.callout-tip}
You now know the **whole structure** of OOP: classes, objects, and the four pillars that make it powerful.
:::

## What comes next

You can now **read** and **design** real object-oriented programs.

::: {.callout-note}
To keep growing, practise:

- Modelling real things as classes (a `Library`, a `Game`, a `Shop`)
- Building small **class families** with inheritance
- Letting a loop work over many types with polymorphism
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
The best way to learn OOP is to **build** with it. Take a small idea and turn it into classes. 🚀
:::

---

<div style="text-align:center; margin-top: 80px; line-height: 1.6;">
  <h1 style="font-size: 3em; font-weight: 700; color: #C41E3A;">Thank You</h1>
  <div style="border-bottom: 3px solid #C41E3A; width: 30%; margin: 24px auto;"></div>
  <h3 style="font-weight: 700; color: #050E3C; margin-top: 30px;">MIN Sothearith</h3>
  <h4 style="font-weight: 400; color: #666;">Phnom Penh International University</h4>
</div>
